sections.title: -군요/-는군요 (Realization) and -네요 (Mild Surprise)
sections.explanation: -군요 and -는군요 express a realization or newfound understanding — 'oh, I see that...' or 'so it is...' The speaker has just come to understand something. With adjectives and 이다: stem + -군요 (e.g., 예쁘군요 — oh, it's pretty). With action verbs (present): stem + -는군요 (e.g., 먹는군요 — oh, you're eating). With past tense: -았/었군요 (e.g., 갔군요 — oh, you went). -네요 expresses mild surprise or an impression upon directly experiencing something — 'oh!' or 'wow.' It is less reflective than -군요 and more about immediate reaction. With verbs/adjectives: stem + -네요 (e.g., 맛있네요 — oh, it's delicious!, 비가 오네요 — oh, it's raining!). -군요 feels like internal realization; -네요 feels like outward reaction to something you just noticed.
sections.tip: -군요 = 'I now realize/understand that...' (reflective). -네요 = 'Oh! I just noticed that...' (reactive, immediate). Both add emotional color to observations.

examples.en: Oh, I see you speak Korean well!

exercises.prompt: You just realized your friend can cook well. Which ending fits best?
exercises.explanation: -는군요 expresses realization about an ongoing fact: 요리를 잘하는군요 = 'Oh, I see you cook well!' (I've come to realize this).

exercises.prompt: You taste soup for the first time and it's spicy. Which ending fits best?
exercises.explanation: -네요 is used for immediate reactions upon experiencing something: 맵네요! = 'Oh, it's spicy!' (just tasted it and reacting).

exercises.prompt: 벌써 졸업했___. (Oh, you already graduated. — realization)
exercises.explanation: Past tense + -군요: 졸업했 + 군요 = 졸업했군요 (Oh, I see you graduated).

sections.title: -겠- (Conjecture and Intention)
sections.explanation: -겠- is an infix that expresses either the speaker's conjecture/supposition or intention/determination. For conjecture: 내일 비가 오겠어요 (It will probably rain tomorrow), 힘들겠어요 (That must be hard), 맛있겠다! (That looks/must be delicious!). The speaker is guessing based on available information. For intention (first person): 제가 하겠습니다 (I will do it — firm determination), 알겠습니다 (I understand / understood — acknowledging). -겠- conjugates like a regular ending: 가겠어요, 먹겠습니다, 좋겠네요. In formal/business contexts, -겠- with first person shows strong will: 최선을 다하겠습니다 (I will do my best). With second/third person, it is always conjecture, never intention.
sections.tip: First person + -겠- = intention ('I will'). Second/third person + -겠- = conjecture ('you/they probably will'). 알겠습니다 (understood) and 맛있겠다 (looks delicious) are extremely common set phrases.

examples.en: The weather will probably be nice tomorrow.

exercises.explanation: 알겠습니다 is first person + -겠-, so it expresses intention/acknowledgment: 'I understand / Understood.' This is one of the most common formal expressions.

exercises.prompt: Your friend shows you a photo of a beautiful beach. How would you react using -겠-?
exercises.explanation: 좋겠다! expresses conjecture/longing: 'That must be nice!' or 'How nice (that would be)!' -겠- here conveys imagining how nice it is.

exercises.prompt: 많이 바쁘시___. (You must be very busy.)
exercises.explanation: -겠어요 expresses conjecture about someone else: 바쁘시겠어요 = You must be busy.

sections.title: -더라고요 (Retrospective) and -잖아요 (Obviously)
sections.explanation: -더라고요 is used to report something you personally witnessed or experienced in the past — 'I noticed that...' or 'I saw that...' It conveys firsthand observation being recalled. For example: 그 식당 음식이 맛있더라고요 (I found that restaurant's food was delicious — when I went there). 어제 비가 많이 오더라고요 (It was raining a lot yesterday — I saw it myself). The speaker is sharing a personal past observation. Important: you generally cannot use -더라고요 about your own actions or states (since you don't 'observe' yourself). -잖아요 means 'you know' or 'obviously' — it reminds the listener of something they should already know or presents something as self-evident. 오늘 일요일이잖아요 (It's Sunday, you know), 제가 말했잖아요 (I told you, didn't I?). It can sound slightly reproachful.
sections.tip: -더라고요 = 'When I was there / at that time, I noticed...' (firsthand past observation). -잖아요 = 'As you know...' or 'obviously...' (reminding someone of a known fact).

examples.en: That movie was really fun. (I saw it and found it so.)
Seoul winters are cold. (I experienced it firsthand.)
I told you yesterday! (you should know this)

exercises.prompt: You went to a new cafe and the coffee was great. How do you share this experience?
exercises.explanation: -더라고요 reports a personal past experience: 커피가 맛있더라고요 = 'The coffee was good (I found when I tried it).' This shares your firsthand observation.

exercises.prompt: Your friend forgot that the library is closed on Mondays. How do you remind them?
exercises.explanation: -잖아요 reminds someone of something they should already know: 도서관이 쉬잖아요 = 'The library is closed, you know!' (obvious/known fact).

exercises.prompt: 제가 전에 ___. (I told you before! — reminding)
exercises.hint: Reminding ending meaning 'you know/obviously' with 말하다 past tense
exercises.explanation: -잖아요 with past tense 말했-: 말했잖아요 = I told you, didn't I!
